sections.heading: Your First Program
content: In the programming world, it's a tradition to display 'Hello, World!' as your first program when learning a new language.
Through this simple program, you can experience the basic flow of writing and running code.

sections.heading: What is the print Function?
content: In Python, you can use the print() function to display text and numbers on the screen.
When you write what you want to display inside the parentheses, that content will appear in the output.

sections.heading: How to Write Strings
content: When displaying strings (text), you need to enclose them in double quotes (") or single quotes (').
Either works the same way, but it's recommended to use them consistently.

examples.title: Basic Usage
examples.explanation: Display a string enclosed in double quotes.

examples.title: Single Quotes Work Too
examples.explanation: Single quotes work the same way.

examples.title: Display Multiple Lines
examples.explanation: Multiple print statements will each display on a new line.

examples.title: Display Numbers
examples.explanation: Numbers can be displayed without quotes. You can also display calculation results.

exercise.task: Write a program to display 'Hello, World!'
instructions: Inside the parentheses, write 'Hello, World!' enclosed in double quotes
Don't forget to close the parentheses!
hints: Inside the print() parentheses, write text enclosed in double quotes

tests.name: Test 1: Display Hello, World!
tests.description: Check if 'Hello, World!' is displayed correctly
tests.code:
import sys
from io import StringIO

# Capture standard output
old_stdout = sys.stdout
sys.stdout = StringIO()

try:
    exec(user_code)
    output = sys.stdout.getvalue()
finally:
    sys.stdout = old_stdout

# Check output
expected = "Hello, World!\n"
if output == expected:
    result = {"passed": True, "message": "✓ Correct!"}
else:
    result = {"passed": False, "message": f"✗ Output is different.\nExpected: {repr(expected)}\nActual: {repr(output)}"}

result
